title: What is an interface
instruction: An interface is like a library card catalog. It lists what you need to *do* — not who you need to *be*.\n"Must be able to `Title() string`" — any type that has that method qualifies. Book? Yes. Magazine? Yes.\nRun this — both Book and Magazine satisfy the `Media` interface because they both have `Title()`.
successMessage: Book says its title, Magazine says its title — but `printTitle()` didn't care which one it got. It just asked: "Can you Title()?" That's the power of interfaces.

title: Define and satisfy an interface
instruction: Your turn. Define a `Media` interface that requires `Pages() int`.\nThen make both `Book` and `Audiobook` satisfy it by adding `Pages()` methods.\nIf it has `Pages()`, it's Media. Period.
hint: type Media interface { Pages() int }  func (b Book) Pages() int { return b.PageCount }  func (a Audiobook) Pages() int { return a.LengthMinutes / 2 }

codeChecks.message: Define the Media interface: type Media interface { ... }
The interface must require Pages() int.
Add Pages() method to Book.
Add Pages() method to Audiobook.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Two different types, one interface. Go doesn't care about inheritance or class trees — just "do you have the method?"

title: ahha — a slice of interfaces treats everything the same
instruction: Because all three media types satisfy `Media`, you can store them in a `[]Media` and treat them uniformly.\nAdd an `Audiobook` to the media slice, then loop through and call `describe` on each.

codeChecks.message: Add Audiobook{TitleName: "Atomic Habits"} to the media slice.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Three different types in one slice, one loop, one function. This is the power of interfaces.

title: The Stringer interface
instruction: Remember `String()` from the Methods chapter? That was an interface all along.\n`fmt.Stringer` requires one method: `String() string`. Any type with that method gets printed beautifully by `fmt.Println`.\nAdd `String()` to `Book` so it prints as `"The Great Gatsby" by F. Scott Fitzgerald`.
hint: func (b Book) String() string { return fmt.Sprintf("\"%s\" by %s", b.Title, b.Author) }
expectedOutput: "The Great Gatsby" by F. Scott Fitzgerald

codeChecks.message: Add String() string method to Book.
Use fmt.Sprintf inside String() to format the output.
Replace the TODO comment with your method.

successMessage: You satisfied an interface without even importing it. fmt just checked: "Does this type have String()?" Yes → use it.

title: Type assertion
instruction: Sometimes you have an interface value and you need the concrete type underneath.\n`concrete, ok := interfaceVal.(ConcreteType)` — safe version with boolean check.\nRun this. Then add a type assertion to check if `m` is an `Audiobook` and print its narrator.
hint: if a, ok := m.(Audiobook); ok { fmt.Println("Narrated by:", a.Narrator) }

codeChecks.message: Add a type assertion: if a, ok := m.(Audiobook); ok { ... }
Print the Audiobook's Narrator.
Replace the TODO comment with your assertion.

successMessage: The two-value assertion (value, ok) is the safe version. Never use the one-value version without ok — it panics if the type is wrong.

title: Type switch
instruction: When you need to handle multiple concrete types from an interface, a type switch is cleaner than chaining type assertions.\n`switch v := i.(type) { case int: ... case string: ... }`\nAdd a `case Magazine` to the type switch that prints the issue number.
expectedOutput: Audiobook: "The Hobbit" narrated by Andy Serkis

codeChecks.message: Add case Magazine: to the type switch.
Print the Magazine's Issue number using v.
Replace the TODO comment with your case.

successMessage: Type switch: one construct, multiple types, zero repetition. Much cleaner than three consecutive if-else type assertions.

title: The empty interface (any)
instruction: `interface{}` (or `any` in newer Go) has zero methods — which means *every* type satisfies it.\nUseful for generic containers, but use sparingly — you lose type safety.\nCall checkIn with `Book{Title: "Go Syntax"}` and see how `any` accepts it too.

codeChecks.message: Call checkIn(Book{Title: "Go Syntax"}).
Replace the TODO comment with your call.

successMessage: The empty interface accepts everything. Powerful, but it's like a shelf labeled "miscellaneous" — you'll need type assertions to figure out what's actually on it.

title: Composing interfaces
instruction: Interfaces can embed other interfaces. The composed interface requires all methods from all embedded interfaces.\n`type ReadWriter interface { Reader; Writer }` — this is how Go's standard library is built.\nRun this. `Borrowable` embeds both `Stockable` and `Returnable`. `LibraryItem` must implement all three methods.
expectedOutput: "The Art of War" returned — 4 copies available
successMessage: LibraryItem satisfies Borrowable by implementing both Stock and Return. Interface composition lets you build precise, minimal contracts.

title: Implicit satisfaction — no implements keyword
instruction: This is the most important thing about Go interfaces. In Java, you write `implements Speaker`. In Go, you just... have the method.\nNo keyword. No declaration. If your type has `String()`, it *is* a Stringer.\nRun this — `LoudBook` never mentions Stringer, but it works.
expectedOutput: READ The Hobbit BY J.R.R. Tolkien! IT IS THE BEST BOOK EVER!
The librarian says: READ The Hobbit BY J.R.R. Tolkien! IT IS THE BEST BOOK EVER!
successMessage: No `implements`, no `extends`, no ceremony. Just methods. This is why Go interfaces are so flexible.

title: Fix the broken interface
instruction: Bug hunt time! The code tries to use `DVD` as a `Media`, but it won't compile.\nRead the error, figure out what's missing, and fix it.
hint: DVD needs a Title() string method. Add: func (d DVD) Title() string { return d.Name }

codeChecks.message: Add Title() string method to DVD.
DVD's Title() should return a string starting with "DVD:".

successMessage: DVD was missing the `Title()` method. No method = doesn't satisfy the interface. The compiler catches this for you.

title: Build a library checkout system
instruction:
No starter code. Build a library checkout system from scratch:
1. Define a `LibraryItem` interface: `Checkout(cardNumber string) string`
2. Create three types: `Book`, `Magazine`, `Audiobook`
3. Each prints its own format (see expected output)
4. Write a loop that calls Checkout on a `[]LibraryItem` with card number "12345"
hint: func (b Book) Checkout(card string) string { return fmt.Sprintf("Book \"%s\" checked out on card %s", b.Title, card) }
expectedOutput: Audiobook "Atomic Habits" checked out on card 12345

codeChecks.message: Define a LibraryItem interface.
The LibraryItem interface must have Checkout(cardNumber string) string.
Define a Book struct.
Define a Magazine struct.
Define an Audiobook struct.
Create a []LibraryItem slice.
Loop through the LibraryItem slice with range.
Call .Checkout("12345") on each item.

successMessage: Three library item types, one interface, one loop. Add a fourth type later? Just implement Checkout(). Nothing else changes.
